coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposites based on quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 ternary alloy nanoparticles and decorated with poly-L-cysteine film for the electrocatalytic application of hydroquinone sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo
She, Nana
Du, Jing
Zhang, Meng
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A mildly one-pot method is developed for the synthesis of quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 ternary alloy nanoparticles (TANPs) at room temperature through the co-reduction of AuCl4 -, PtCl6 - and Co2+ with hydrazine hydrate. Characterizations of XRD, XPS, HRTEM, EDS and SAED successfully reveal the crystal structure, composition, valence and morphology of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, respectively. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs with good dispersion and multi-density surface defects occupies the optimal electrochemical active surface area (ECSA). After the coated poly-L-cysteine (P-L-Cys) film on the Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE surface, the morphology, element mapping and surface roughness of the P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE are investigated via FESEM and AFM to verify continuous electrode modification processes. The electrochemical behaviors of the composite electrode for hydroquinone (HQ) are evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) with interfacial properties of adsorption and diffusion. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) for HQ electrochemical sensing at 0.10 V (vs. SCE) exhibits two linear response ranges from 0.1 to 30 and 30–200 μM, respectively. A low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.045 μM is obtained with a sensitivity of 4.247 μA μM−1·cm−2. The resulting P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE also presents ascendant selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and stability. In addition, the established method is applied to the assessment of the HQ level in real water samples (mineral water, tap water and lake water) with the satisfactory results of spiked recoveries. The sensor may become a promising tool for the trace analysis of the electroactive substance in food or environmental samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns from Au2Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au1Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au0.5Pt1Co1 TANPs and Pt1Co1 ANPs; XPS spectra of (B) Au, (C) Pt and (D) Co element from the quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs.
HRTEM and SAED images from Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs.

(A) EIS of different modified electrodes in 0.2 M KNO3 containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6], inset: the fitted equivalent circuit including solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), Warburg impedance (Wd) and constant phase element (Q); (B) CVs on different modified electrodes at 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 6.98) with 100 μM HQ.
FESEM images of (A) Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE, (B) P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE and the element mapping images of Au, Pt, Co and S on P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE.

(A) DPVs of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE in phosphate buffer (0.1 M pH = 6.98) with various concentrations of HQ (a → m: 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 70, 100, 120, 150, 180, 200 μM), inset: the calibrating plot of HQ; (B) Selectivity from the P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE including 100 μM HQ with the same concentration of CC and RS, the 5-fold concentration of AA and UA, and 10-fold concentration of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, NO3 -, Cl− and PO4 3−, respectively.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposites based on quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 ternary alloy nanoparticles and decorated with poly-L-cysteine film for the electrocatalytic application of hydroquinone sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A mildly one-pot method is developed for the synthesis of quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 ternary alloy nanoparticles (TANPs) at room temperature through the co-reduction of AuCl4 -, PtCl6 - and Co2+ with hydrazine hydrate. Characterizations of XRD, XPS, HRTEM, EDS and SAED successfully reveal the crystal structure, composition, valence and morphology of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, respectively. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs with good dispersion and multi-density surface defects occupies the optimal electrochemical active surface area (ECSA). After the coated poly-L-cysteine (P-L-Cys) film on the Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE surface, the morphology, element mapping and surface roughness of the P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE are investigated via FESEM and AFM to verify continuous electrode modification processes. The electrochemical behaviors of the composite electrode for hydroquinone (HQ) are evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) with interfacial properties of adsorption and diffusion. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) for HQ electrochemical sensing at 0.10 V (vs. SCE) exhibits two linear response ranges from 0.1 to 30 and 30–200 μM, respectively. A low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.045 μM is obtained with a sensitivity of 4.247 μA μM−1·cm−2. The resulting P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE also presents ascendant selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and stability. In addition, the established method is applied to the assessment of the HQ level in real water samples (mineral water, tap water and lake water) with the satisfactory results of spiked recoveries. The sensor may become a promising tool for the trace analysis of the electroactive substance in food or environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroquinone (HQ), a kind of disubstituted phenol, has been generally used in various fields including drug synthesis, cosmetics, textile, photography, oil-reﬁnery, pesticides, and antioxidants in rubber and food industry (Renganathan et al., 2019; Umar et al., 2020). It is well-known that HQ with toxicity and degradation-resistant is inevitably diffused into the water and soil in the process of industrial application. High concentration levels of HQ can give rise to nausea, headache, tinnitus and kidney damage in humans, even at trace level concentrations, it will also affect human health (Huang et al., 2020). According to authoritative reports, HQ has been classified as a hazardous and toxic material through the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), meanwhile, it has been labeled as a category 3 carcinogen and mutagen via the European Commission (EC) (Balram et al., 2019).

Based on the water pollution and the harmful effect of HQ, it is extremely necessary to exploit a reliable, sensitive, rapid and simple analytical method for HQ determination in drinking water and domestic water. Currently, a series of analytical methods have been reported for HQ detection, such as colorimetry, chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, spectrophotometry, fluorescence and photoluminescence. Compared to the above methods, the electrochemical method (electrochemical sensor, ECS) has aroused widespread attention, due to high sensitivity, fast response, timesaving, real-time analysis, convenient operation and low-cost device (Asadian et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2014). Due to the electroactive group of hydroxyl in the cyclobenzene, the electrochemical oxidation of HQ is feasible. Unfortunately, the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) can hardly accurately determinate HQ content, owing to the overlapped oxidation potentials from the isomers of HQ (catechol and resorcinol) and the fouling from the HQ oxidation. To address this issue, massive efforts have been dedicated to the development of novel sensing materials including metal nanoparticles, metal oxide, carbon nanomaterials, polymer membranes and their composites. However, it is still essential to improve the low-level determination of HQ with the first-class selectivity and stability for food and environmental safety. Hence, the further design and development of excellent electrocatalytic materials are a challenging endeavor.

Noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), especially for platinum (Pt), are widely investigative nanomaterials, owing to inimitably physicochemical properties. However, the expensive price and barren resources hinder the large-scale utilization of Pt nanomaterials. To lower the cost and enhance potential applications, a series of strategies containing nanostructured tailoring, size control and compositional variation have been carried out (Chen and P.Holt-Hindle, 2010; Radhakrishnan et al., 2020; Suganya et al., 2019). Among them, alloy nanomaterials with Pt have aroused wide concern, because of excellent electronic conductivity and good synergistic effect at the atomic level, which are originated from combining with the advantages of multiple compositions (Gilroy et al., 2016; Thanh et al., 2017). At present, Pt-based alloy nanomaterials with another noble/base metals (Au, Ru, Pd, Rh, Ir, Co, Ni, Fe, Cu, etc.) have been extensively explored and hold great promise as efficient nanocarriers and catalysts (Hien et al., 2018; Song et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the tremendous advance has been achieved in tuning the composition, size, morphology, and architecture at nanoscale for promoting electrochemical performance (Douk et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2015). Cobalt (Co) as a 3d transition metal incorporated with Pt has revealed a superbly synergic catalytic effect on various electrochemical reactions. Yang et al. (2019) utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal method synthesized a type of hybrids from PtCo nanocubes holden on the reduced graphene oxide (PtCo@rGO). The specific morphology and well dispersion are beneficial to provide more active sites (eg. edges and corners) on the surface to increase the catalytic activity. A novel synthesis strategy on Pt3Co zigzag nanowires (z-NWs) with a Pt-skin of the high-index facet has been reported by Luo and co-workers (Luo et al., 2018). The electrode modified via such specific structure exhibits remarkable electrocatalytic performance with regard to hydrogen peroxide, hydrazine hydrate, dopamine and acetaminophen, respectively, which is attributed to atomic-level tuning at the near-/surface structures of Pt-skin Pt3Co z-NWs. Zhao et al. (2016) employing an effective and facile ultrasonic electrodeposition means prepared a layer of high-density, ultraﬁne and finely disseminated binary PtCo alloy nanoparticles on the graphene paper fabricated with a 3D nanoporous gold. The active sites of PtCo alloy nanoparticles display outstanding electrochemical sensing performance for glucose detection. This is chiefly attributed to bifunctional and electronic effects between Pt and Co atoms. The PtCo alloy with hierarchical nanoporous architecture was presented on the basis of selectively etching Al element from PtCoAl alloy under a mild alkaline condition (Xu et al., 2013a). The obtained nanoporous PtCo is a 3D network-like nanostructure and a narrow size distribution is around 3 nm. The roughly porous surface can create a high speciﬁc surface area with countless active sites, which is easy to facilitate the mass and electron transport of analytes. Other morphologies including hollow PtCo alloy nanochains (Bai et al., 2011; Peng et al., 2012), mesoporous PtCo nanospheres (Ou et al., 2019) and Co@Pt core-shell (Mei et al., 2015), have been also investigated in detail, indicating that the performance of ECS and the post-decoration of the electrode surface highly depend on the regular geometric structure. Although the detection of analytes can be implemented on the modified electrodes of PtCo sensing materials with various morphologies, these methods still have some shortcomings (for instance: complex preparation, the narrow linear range of concentrations and not quite outstanding selectivity reproducibility, stability and durability), all of which can impede their further application. In order to obtain better properties, ternary alloy nanomaterials with higher electrocatalytic activity and chemical stability have attracted considerable concern (Mao et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2018). Here, Au1.5Pt1Co1 ternary alloy nanoparticles (TANPs) were prepared at room temperature. The introduction of Au element with their excellent biocompatibilities are considered to be an incomparable choice, which can be easy to modify biomolecules such as amino acid, protein, enzyme, antibody, antigen and so on (Wang et al., 2017b; Xiao et al., 2020). Non-noble metal (Co) doped is capable of noteworthy reducing the preparation cost of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, meanwhile, the componential tuning from Au, Pt and Co elements is beneficial to improve their electrocatalytic performance, in which the exposed multiple reaction sites are apt to form appropriate binding configurations for a reactant or intermediate (Liu et al., 2015). What's more, the facile and convenient synthetic route of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs without high-temperature reaction or calcination is also suitable for relatively large-scale manufacturing and application.

The immobilization of biological molecules (amino acids) on the surface of the electrode via electropolymerization is a promising way, due to controllable thin ﬁlm, the ease of fabrication, and forming the multifunctional layer with the existence of functional groups (Zhang et al., 2020). L-Cysteine (L-Cys) as a non-essential amino acid plays an important role in biological systems (Arvand et al., 2018). Poly-L-cysteine (P-L-Cys) film can be acquired easily by electropolymerization, because of its monomer containing amine (-NH2), sulfhydryl (-SH) and carboxyl (-COOH) functional groups. Some literature have reported that the sensor with the decoration of P-L-Cys film displays excellent electrocatalytic performance for the targeted molecules with hydroxyl functional groups (Ma et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015, 2018). This is due to the fact that the P-L-Cys film can provide more active sites and improve sensor sensitivity. Meanwhile, the distribution change of surface charge as well as the good stability and strong adherence from the prepared P-L-Cys film is able to boost the selectivity and stability of the sensor (He et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2019).

In this paper, the quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs with the controllable morphology are synthesized by means of a one-pot method. Subsequently, P-L-Cys film is one-step constructed on the GCE surface modified by Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that the constructed P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE has applied to HQ electrochemical oxidation with superior sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. The utilization with satisfactory results in practical samples proves the practicability of the sensor.

Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) K30, chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·12H2O), cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), and L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Dimethylacetamide (DMAC), heptanoic acid (C7H14O2), potassium nitrate (KNO3), calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl2·6H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) and cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, 85 wt%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), urea acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), lead dinitrate (Pb(NO3)2) and absolute ethanol were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytically pure and used as received without puriﬁcation. The deionized water (DIW: 18.2 MΩ cm) was manufactured via the Millipore equipment.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were confirmed using a D/Max-2200 X-ray diffractometer with operating at 40 kV and 30 mA in a scan range of 20–90° at the scan speed of 2°·min−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was determined using a Thermo fisher scientific ESCALAB 250Xi with an Al Kα radiation source. Morphology, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs were gained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) from JEOL JEM-2200FS with the acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) from ZEISS GeminiSEM 500 was carried out to acquire the surface morphology and element mapping of the modiﬁed GCE. The images of the atomic force microscope (AFM) based on the tapping mode were acquired on Veeco Dimension 3100.

The whole electrochemical analyses were conducted on an electrochemical workstation CHI760E in a conventional three-electrode setup: a bare or modiﬁed glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Ф = 3 mm), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum electrode serving as the working electrode, the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with the frequency ranging from 0.1 to 100 kHz in 0.2 M KNO3 containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV: the pulse width of 0.2 s, pulse period of 0.5 s and pulse amplitude of 0.05 V) were employed in the above system. The entire electrolytes were deoxidized by means of high-pure N2 flow bubbling for 15 min ahead of each experiment.

200 μL of the mixture, 100 μL H2PtCl6 (0.15 M) and 100 μL Co(NO3)2 (0.15 M), was dropwise added into a 100 mL flask comprising DMAC (5 mL), the pre-made PVP solution (500 μL, 0.1 g mL−1) and heptanoic acid (50 μL). Then, the mixture was kept ultrasonic vibration for 2 min before magnetic stirring for 10 min. 150 μL of HAuCl4 (0.15 M) was pipetted into the aforementioned mixture with magnetic stirring for 10 min. Subsequently, 20 μL of N2H4·H2O (85 wt%) was dripped and the color became blackish brown. The reaction was terminated after magnetic stirring for 30 min. Later, keeping static conditions for 1 day at ambient temperature, the post-synthetic mixture was rinsed with absolute ethanol and centrifuged for a total 4 times (9000 r·min−1, 5 min) to obtain Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs. Other materials were prepared in the same way apart from the molar ratio of H2PtCl6, Co(NO3)2 and HAuCl4, labeled as Pt NPs, Pt1Co1 ANPs, Au NPs, Au1.5Pt1 ANPs, Au0.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au1Pt1Co1 TANPs and Au2Pt1Co1 TANPs, respectively.

Before its usage, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was successively polished by 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina slurry on the polishing pad, followed by thorough rinsing with absolute ethanol and DIW. 10 μL of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs-ethanol suspension (10 mg mL−1) was cast to the bare GCE surface. After the solvent evaporated under infrared light, the Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE was acquired. Other electrodes were modified similarly, named as Pt/GCE, Pt1Co1/GCE, Au/GCE, Au1.5Pt1/GCE, Au0.5Pt1Co1/GCE, Au1Pt1Co1/GCE and Au2Pt1Co1/GCE.

To the fabrication of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE, the pre-prepared Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE was immersed into the phosphate buffer (0.1 M pH = 6.98) with 1 mM of L-Cys, and then CV electropolymerization for 10 cycles with the scan range between −1.5 and 2.0 V at the scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. After flushing with DIW and drying under the N2 stream, the P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE was obtained. The P-L-Cys/GCE was fabricated in an identical way. The electropolymerized CVs of L-Cys are exhibited in Fig. S1 and the corresponding electropolymerization mechanism is given in Scheme 1 .

The XRD patterns of the ANPs samples are exhibited in Fig. 1 A. The five diffraction peaks can be all indexed to be a face-centered cubic crystal without any impurity phase, based on pure Au (JCPDS no. 04–0784) and Pt (JCPDS no. 04–0802). The values of diffraction peaks from Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs are 38.66°, 44.87°, 65.70°, 78.92° and 83.15°, which are corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) facets, respectively. With the introduction of different Au contents, the corresponding lattice parameters are shifted to some extent, indicating that the atoms of Pt and Co enter into the lattice of Au causing the lattice contraction and forming TANPs compared with that of pure Au. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing (d) obtained with 2.32 Å is greater than that of Pt1Co1 (2.25 Å) and less than that of Au (2.35 Å). The strongest diffraction peak at 38.66° illustrates that Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs principally grow along with the orientation of the (111) facet. XPS measurements are implemented to survey the surface composition and chemical valence state of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs. The survey-scan XPS spectrum from Fig. S2 exhibits the XPS peaks of Au, Pt, C, N, O and Co. Among them, C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s) are derived from the elements of the synthesis medium. In Fig. 1B, the deconvolution peaks at (Au 4f7/2: 83.6 eV) and (Au 4f5/2: 87.3 eV) can be vested in Au0. The Pt (4f) spectrum is presented in Fig. 1C. The two intense peaks from 70.7 eV of the Pt (4f7/2) and 74.0 eV of the Pt (4f5/2) are assigned to Pt0, while two relatively weak peaks at 71.8 eV and 75.1 eV are attributed to Pt2+ (Pei et al., 2020). Fig. 1D depicts the Co (2p) spectrum of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs. The Co 2p3/2 peak (780.3 eV) and its satellite (784.7 eV) indicate that the oxidized Co species are existed on the surface of the sample (Zhang et al., 2017). Meanwhile, the emergence of the peak from 778.3 eV reveals the existence of the Co0 (Song et al., 2016). The above results manifest that the zerovalent states of Au, Pt and Co coexist on the sample surface and further verify the formation of TANPs together with XRD result.

Fig. 2 shows HRTEM images and SAED of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs. Distinctly, the whole morphology with a quasi-networked structure is observed and holds a good dispersity from Fig. 2A. As seen in Fig. 2a, the 5 diffraction rings from SAED are separately in line with (111), (200), (220), (311) and (420) facets of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, illustrating that the Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs have an independent and random growth direction. The lattice spacing of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs is 2.32 Å, as depicted in Fig. 2b, which is in line with that of (111) facet. Some surface defects such as stacking faults, dislocations and twin boundaries are also observed from Fig. 2c–e. These exposed surface defects are usually acknowledged as remarkably active centers and can promote the adsorption and catalytic effects of nanomaterials (Xie et al., 2019). Furthermore, the EDS spectrum in Fig. S3 testifies the atomic proportion of Au, Pt and Co with 55: 34: 11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of Pt/GCE, Pt1Co1/GCE, Au/GCE, Au1.5Pt1/GCE, Au0.5Pt1Co1/GCE, Au1Pt1Co1/GCE, Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE and Au2Pt1Co1/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: CVs with the scan potential scope of −0.2 to 1.2 V at H2SO4 solution (0.5 M) is shown in Fig. S4. In addition to Au/GCE, the decorated electrodes all display hydrogen adsorption/desorption areas within a range from −0.2 to 0.1 V. The obvious reduction peaks are presented at about 0.4 V, which are resulted from the reduction of PtOx, PtCoOx, AuPtOx and AuPtCoOx. Meanwhile, the reduction peak of AuOx is observed at ca. 0.8 V (see the inset of Fig. S4A). In order to evaluate their electrochemical activities, the calculated formula of the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is given in the supplementary material. In Fig. S4A, the ECSAs of Pt NPs, Pt1Co1 ANPs, Au1.5Pt1 ANPs and Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs are approximately 7.1, 8.4, 17.7 and 38.4 m2 g−1, respectively. The outcome demonstrates that the elements of Co and Au introduced are beneficial to improving the ECSA, due to the strong synergic and ensemble effect at the atomic level, which can improve the utilization of Pt. As depicted in Fig. S4B, the resulting ECSAs of Au0.5Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au1Pt1Co1 TANPs, Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs and Au2Pt1Co1 TANPs are in turn ca. 12.2, 20.6, 38.4 and 30.3 m2 g−1, suggesting that the increase of Au content is apt to facilitate the electrocatalytic activity of TANPs. However, the excess of Au content can lead to the reduction of ECSA, resulting from the active sites of Pt covered by Au. As a result, the GCE modified via Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs with the maximal ECSA is selected for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
EIS as an effective means can not only uncover the layer-by-layer assembly of the modified electrode but also analyze the electrode materials and study the process kinetics. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the whole of the Nyquist plot had two parts: i) the linear segment of lower frequencies from a controlled diffusion process; ii) the semicircle segment of higher frequencies from the electron transfer limited process or the electron transfer resistance (R
ct ). It can be seen that the GCE modified via Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs only exhibited a straight line, demonstrating that the diffusion process is predominant. After the individual decoration of the P-L-Cys film on the GCE, the obtained electron transfer resistance of P-L-Cys/GCE (R
ct = 385.3 Ω) is larger than that of GCE (R
ct = 231.4 Ω), indicating that the electron transfer capability for P-L-Cys/GCE is inferior to that for GCE. However, the established P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE possesses a minimum semicircle and the value of R
ct is 198.1 Ω. The result illustrates that Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs introduced supplies strong electron conduction tunnel, which makes P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE easier for the electron transfer and facilitates the electrical conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of HQ at different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the electrochemical oxidation of HQ with different electrodes, CVs are implemented in 100 μM HQ phosphate buffer (0.1 M pH = 6.98) at the scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. As expressed in Fig. 3B, all electrodes display a pair of redox peaks, owing to the electrocatalytic oxidation of the phenolic hydroxyl group from HQ, in which the HQ molecule is oxidized to p-benzoquinone. Among them, P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE appears to the highest electrocatalytic activity towards HQ, attributing to the synergistic effect from Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs and P-L-Cys film. As shown in Figs. S5 and S6, FESEM and AFM results present the relative change in the electrode surface before and after assembly with the measured roughness (R
a ) values of (A) bare GCE (6.40 nm), (B) P-L-Cys/GCE (2.34 nm), (C) Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE (22.68 nm) and (D) P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE (14.37 nm), respectively. The GCE decorated by Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs shows the maximum roughness, which can offer a relatively large surface area as well as many active centers to electropolymerize L-Cys. After the electropolymerization of L-Cys on the Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE, the R
a value is reduced to some extent, nonetheless, the morphology of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE in Fig. 4 B still maintains a certain surface roughness with multiple pores in comparison with that of Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE in Fig. 4A. The element mapping images of Au, Pt, Co and characteristic S element from Fig. 4 exhibit the even distribution of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs and P-L-Cys film. The surface morphology like this is advantageous to allow the facile accessibility of the electrode/electrolyte interface and accelerate electron transfer in the reaction (Arvand et al., 2018; Arvand and Sayyar Ardaki, 2017). In addition, Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE has yet to be exhibited the superior anodic peak current, mainly thanks to its surface with the strong adsorption effect hardly removing contaminants from the HQ oxidation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of HQ on P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the mass transfer to the electrode surface is strongly inﬂuenced by the change in the acidity of the electrolyte, it is of vital importance to examine the impact of pH. Fig. S7A depicts CVs of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE with various pH values ranging from 5.0 to 8.0 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer containing 100 μM HQ at the scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. The anodic peak current initially increases with the addition of pH from 5 to 6.98, due to the HQ molecule on the adsorption area of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE replaced by the high concentration of protons in the electrolyte medium. Then, with pH rising from 6.98 to 8, the anodic peak current decreases, which is due to protons deﬁciency at higher pH values (Balram et al., 2019). Therefore, the optimal pH value emerges in pH = 6.98 at the 0.1 M phosphate buffer and is applied to the following experiments. As seen in the inset of Fig. S7A, the anodic peak potential (E
pa ) emerges a decreasing trend with the pH value increased and has a good linear relationship within the range from 5.0 to 8.0. The equation of linear regression is expressed as E
pa (V) = −0.058 pH + 0.514 (R
2 = 0.9954). According to the Nernst equation (Yin et al., 2020):
d
E p
/ d p H =
(
2 . 303 m RT
)
/
(
n F
)
where m is the number of protons; R represents the gas constant (8.314 J K−1·mol−1); T is the temperature (298 K); n denotes the number of electrons; F is the Faraday constant (96500 C mol−1). Based on the slope of dEp/dpH is a constant (0.058 V·pH−1), the m/n value is figured out as 0.98 (≈1). The result indicates that the equal transfer number of electrons and protons participates in the reaction of HQ oxidation (Qi et al., 2019).

As seen in Fig. S7B, the CVs of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE based on various scan rates from 0.01 to 0.3 V s−1 are scrutinized at 100 μM HQ phosphate buffer (0.1 M pH = 6.98). A series of well-defined redox peaks progressively increase with the enhancement of the scan rate and the anodic peak potential (E
pa ) moves to positive orientation and the cathode peak potential (E
pc ) mobilizes to negative orientation. It can be found from the inset of Fig. S7C that the E
pa and E
pc show a linear correlation with the logarithm of the scan rate (logv) from 0.075 to 0.3 V s−1. The corresponding linear regression equations are formulated as follows, E
pa (V) = 0.009 + 0.050 logv (V·s−1), R
2 = 0.9931; E
pc (V) = 0.075–0.040 logv (V·s−1), R
2 = 0.9842. Combined with the following Laviron formula (Laviron, 1979):
E
p a
=
E
0 ’
+ 2 . 3 RT /
[
( 1 − α )
nF ]
· l o g v
E

=
E
0 ’
– 2 . 3 RT / α nF · l o g v

in which, E0’, R, T, α, n and F are defined to be the formal reduction potential (V), the gas constant (8.314 J K−1·mol−1), the transfer coefﬁcient, the number of electrons and the Faraday constant (96500 C mol−1), respectively. The calculated number of the electron is approximate to 2. The above-mentioned result illustrates that the electrocatalytic oxidation of HQ involves a two-electron reaction on the surface of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE and the speculated reaction mechanism of HQ is presented in Scheme 1. Besides, Fig. S7D also exhibits that the logarithm of the anodic peak current (logI
pa ) and the cathode peak current (logI
pc ) are linear with the logarithm of the scan rate (logv) from 0.075 to 0.3 V s−1. The linear regression equations are logI
pa (μA) = 0.731 + 0.490 logv (V·s−1), R
2 = 0.9989; logI
pc (μA) = 0.788 + 0.494 logv (V·s−1), R
2 = 0.9993, illustrating that the electrocatalytic oxidation of HQ is subjected to both adsorption and diffusion process (Dang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing detection for HQ on P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the current response on varying concentrations of HQ is tested using the DPV technique. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the anodic peak current of HQ at 0.10 V (vs. SCE) is enhanced with the increase in the concentration from 0.1 to 200 μM. The obtained calibration plot from triplicate experiments is expressed in the inset of Fig. 5A, corresponding to two linear regression equations: I
pa (μA) = 0.637 C
HQ (μM) + 4.493 (R
2 = 0.9946, 0.1–30 μM) and I
pa (μA) = 0.204 C
HQ (μM) + 16.981 (R
2 = 0.9985, 30–200 μM). The calculated detection limit is 0.045 μM, based on 3 times the background current noise level and the corresponding sensitivity is identified as 4.247 μA μM−1·cm−2. The constructed sensor showing different linear relationships in diverse concentration ranges is probably due to the following factors (Dong et al., 2015): at the low levels of HQ, the local concentration at the sensor surface is quickly exhausted and converted into the product, leading to the electrode response with high sensitivity. However, at higher HQ levels, the fouling probability of the electrode surface from the reaction products is increased and the saturation level is approached at a higher concentration of HQ, all of which can result in a relatively low slope. Besides, a related summary of typical HQ sensors is introduced in Table 1 . It reveals that the proposed sensor with excellent performance in this work is predominant for the determination of HQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and stability of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5B, the latent influences of in-/organic substances for the HQ determination are investigated. The selected interfering matters may coexist with HQ in practical samples, which contain the same concentration of CC and RS, the 5-fold concentration of AA and UA, and 10-fold concentration of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, NO3 -, Cl− and PO4 3−, respectively. It is found that the interfering species are almost no influence for HQ detection at 0.10 V (vs. SCE), because of the deviation with less than 5%. The corresponding recovery values of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE range from 97.6 to 102.4%. Moreover, the anodic peak potentials of the isomeride from HQ, CC and RS in Fig. S8 are 0.10, 0.21 and 0.66 V, respectively, illustrating the presence of characteristic peaks at specific voltages without any overlap. Fig. S9 exhibits that the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.1% based on eight successive measurements. For ten different electrodes (see in Fig. S10), the RSD is calculated to be 3.9%. The anodic peak current of HQ still retains 94% of its initial current response after P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE was placed at 4 °C for over 15 days. All of the results manifest that P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE has favorable selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility and stability.

To assess the practical use of P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE, mineral water, tap water and lake water are chosen as sample analysis and no target analyte (HQ) is found in the practical sample. The standard addition method is employed to quantify HQ content. The given concentration of HQ is spiked to a certain volume of sample, then different concentrations of HQ are measured by DPV. As described in Table S1, the recoveries are in the scope of 90.5–103.9% and RSD is below 3.7%, which indicate to the excellent potential for the real-time application of HQ detection in practical samples.

An innovative electrochemical sensor of HQ has been developed successfully on the basis of the quasi-networked Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs modified by P-L-Cys film. The use of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs not only provides a relatively rough and large surface area but also the exposed surface defects offer more active sites, meanwhile, the alloying and ensemble effect at the atomic level effectively facilitate the charge transfer. After the decoration of the P-L-Cys film, P-L-Cys/Au1.5Pt1Co1/GCE exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity, long-term stability and satisfactory repeatability and reproducibility towards HQ electrocatalytic response, resulting from the synergistic effect of Au1.5Pt1Co1 TANPs and P-L-Cys film. The quantification of HQ with reliable results in practical samples also testifies the excellent application prospect of the sensor. Besides, such strategies from the TANPs preparation as well as the construction of biomolecular polymer film have a certain referential significance for the development of other sensors.
